text: "You alone we worship, and You alone we ask for help" marks a turn from speaking about God to speaking directly to Him. Worship ('ibadah) and seeking help (isti'anah) are joined: sincere servitude is itself the doorway to divine aid, and the plural 'we' situates the worshipper within the community of believers rather than in isolation.

The request 'Guide us to the straight path' is the heart of the surah: having affirmed worship, the servant asks for the one thing every act depends on — guidance. The straight path (al-sirat al-mustaqim) is the unwavering way that leads directly to God, contrasted in the next verse with the paths of those who provoked wrath and those who strayed.

"Say: He is Allah, the One (ahad)" declares pure divine unity (tawhid). 'Ahad' negates any internal multiplicity or composition in God's essence — He is One in a sense no created thing can share. The surah is reported to equal a third of the Quran because it concentrates the doctrine of tawhid that the whole revelation expounds.
